title: Identify influential points
description: Practice identifying high-leverage points and outliers and thinking about how those points are influential in regression analysis.

assessment_items.question_content:
Moussa was curious which points in the scatterplot below were most influential in terms of the slope of the regression line.

![A scatterplot has horizontal axis, x, which ranges from 0 to 80, in increments of 20; and vertical axis, y, which ranges from 0 to 100, in increments of 20. Point A is graphed at (38, 36). Point B is graphed at (32, 27). Point C is graphed at (47, 46). Point D is graphed at (62, 58). 9 individual data points are plotted as follows. (38, 36), (32, 27), (47, 46), (62, 58), (41, 38), (44, 36), (45, 47), (41, 34), and (71, 59). A line rises through (0, 20) and (55, 50). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/160d7cd1e11d0cf2f14c81fa326271e839955175)

**Which point, if removed, would cause the slope to become the steepest?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The data already show a positive slope. So to make it steeper, we need to make the slope even more positive.

We can remove a point that is pulling the left end of the regression line upward or a point that is pulling the right end of the regression line downward.

Which of the points shown is pulling an end of the regression line the most?

Point $\purpleD{B}$ is far above the left end of the regression line. Removing point $\purpleD{B}$ would pivot the left end of the line lower and the right end higher.

With point $\purpleD{B}$:

![The same scatterplot as above.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7d1cb83f5a78caeb2e4c80ff8abe09e4cc4c5c8a)

Without point $\purpleD{B}$:

![A scatterplot with the same points as above, except without point B. A line rises through (15, 20) and (55, 50). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/aef94cd5715c104e285eefb5d4cfac1d7912c924)

[[☃ explanation 1]]
If we removed point $\purpleD{B}$, the slope would become the steepest.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jama was curious which points in the scatterplot below were most influential in terms of the correlation coefficient, $r$.

![A scatterplot has horizontal axis, x, which ranges from 0 to 80, in increments of 20; and vertical axis, y, which ranges from negative 100 to 10, in increments of 20. Point A is graphed at (55, negative 69). Point B is graphed at (52, negative 42). Point C is graphed at (40, negative 40). Point D is graphed at (43, negative 58). 12 individual data points are plotted as follows. (55, negative 69), (52, negative 42), (40, negative 40), (43, negative 58), (48, negative 33), (60, negative 51), (42, negative 31), (45, negative 46), (46, negative 26), (43, negative 42), (67, negative 75), and (31, negative 42). A line falls through (15, negative 40) and (70, negative 50). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1baaf051e6d1c988d75b2f2901eed91f5df129f7)

**Which point, if removed, would cause $r$ to get closest to $-1$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The correlation coefficient $r$ measures how strong the linear relationship is.

It ranges from $-1$ to $1$. 

- Values near $0$ represent very weak relationships.
- Values near $1$ represent strong positive relationships.
- Values near $-1$ represent strong negative relationships.

The data already show a slight negative relationship. The value of $r$ will decrease, that is, become closer to $-1$, if the relationship becomes stronger.

We can remove a point that doesn't follow the pattern well to make the relationship stronger.

Point $\redE{A}$ is the farthest of the options from the regression line and has the largest residual. Removing point $\redE{A}$ would strengthen the negative relationship the most.

With point $\redE{A}$:

![The same scatterplot as above.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b8be39634ba54fafafb4b7a2b2d04c41fef66b4a)

Without point $\redE{A}$:

![A scatterplot with the same points as above, except without point A. A line falls through (10, negative 31) and (75, negative 60). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d0e2e99117cc20a295a12e4e336f62672682f17d)

[[☃ explanation 1]]
If we removed point $\redE{A}$, the value of $r$ would get the closest to $-1$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kumari was curious which points in the scatterplot below were most influential in terms of the $y$-intercept.

![A scatterplot has horizontal axis, x, which ranges from 0 to 80, in increments of 15; and vertical axis, y, which ranges from negative 35 to 5, in increments of 10. Point A is graphed at (57, negative 26). Point B is graphed at (53, negative 17). Point C is graphed at (38, negative 17). Point D is graphed at (42, negative 25). 12 individual data points are plotted as follows. (57, negative 26), (53, negative 17), (38, negative 17), (42, negative 25), (47, negative 12), (62, negative 20), (41, negative 12), (44, negative 19), (45, negative 9), (41, negative 17), (71, negative 28), and (28, negative 18). A line falls through (0, negative 8) and (60, negative 20). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/adc8537d28246fc82a25fc36f9be00f403cec757)

**Which point, if removed, would cause the $y$-intercept to decrease the most?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The data already show a negative slope and a $y$-intercept of about $-8$. There are two ways we can decrease the $y$-intercept. 

-  We can make the negative slope less steep so the end near the $y$-axis pivots downward.
- We can move the entire regression line downward.

We can remove a point that is:

- rotating one end of the regression line toward itself (left end up or right end down), or
- pulling the middle of the regression line upward.

Which of the points shown is pulling the middle or an end of the regression line the most?

Point $\redE{A}$ is far higher than the pattern would suggest, and it's close to the mean $x$-value. So the entire line (not just one end of the line) gets "pulled" higher to account for this point.

Removing point $\redE{A}$ would let the line drop lower to fit the overall pattern of the remaining points. The $y$-intercept would decrease.

With point $\redE{A}$:

![The same scatterplot as above.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d535fc7e7d8323e15c95b0fdd351dd5c226a4cd7)

Without point $\redE{A}$:

![A scatterplot with the same points as above, except without point A. A line falls through (0,-9) and (75, negative 25). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/23cdd85abde001d1834de4bbc06d8202fbd0d33b)

[[☃ explanation 1]]
If we removed point $\redE{A}$, the $y$-intercept would decrease the most.

assessment_items.question_content:
Himari was curious which points in the scatterplot below were most influential in terms of the coefficient of determination, $r^2$.

![A scatterplot has horizontal axis, x, which ranges from negative 5 to 80, in increments of 10; and vertical axis, y, which ranges from 0 to 300, in increments of 25. Point A is graphed at (57, 215). Point B is graphed at (53, 213). Point C is graphed at (38, 149). Point D is graphed at (42, 156). 8 individual data points are plotted as follows. (57, 215), (53, 213), (38, 149), (42, 156), (47, 192), (62, 247), (41, 165), and (44, 172). A line rises through (25, 99), (40, 150) and (55, 201). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8793120c8640c82761c812209eedbbf478702e25)

**Which point, if removed, would cause $r^2$ to decrease the most?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
R-squared is a number between $0$ and $1$ that measures the percent of variability in the response variable that is accounted for by the least-squares regression line. In other words, stronger linear relationships have higher values of $r^2$, and weaker linear relationships have lower values of $r^2$.

We can remove a point that fits the overall pattern to weaken the relationship.

Which of the points shown, if removed, would weaken the relationship the most?

Point $\maroonD{C}$ is very close to the regression line, so it fits the overall pattern well. 

Removing point $\maroonD{C}$ would weaken the relationship, so $r^2$ would decrease.

With point $\maroonD{C}$:

![The same scatterplot as above.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/727a8ca5a2c035f88e9998b2aff6f4648ef07c92)

Without point $\maroonD{C}$:

![A scatterplot with the same points as above, except without point C. A line rises through (0, 24) and (55, 199). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ad56ded932af4ae8545bc2a0eff1368a10658da8)

[[☃ explanation 1]]
If we removed point $\maroonD{C}$, the value of $r^2$ would decrease the most.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jonas was curious which points in the scatterplot below were most influential in terms of the $y$-intercept.

![A scatterplot has horizontal axis, x, which ranges from 0 to 80, in increments of 10; and vertical axis, y, which ranges from negative 20 to 50, in increments of 10. Point A is graphed at (57, negative 4). Point B is graphed at (53, 10). Point C is graphed at (38, 13). Point D is graphed at (42, 4). 8 individual data points are plotted as follows. (57, negative 4), (53, 10), (38, 13), (42, 4), (47, 15), (62, 4), (41, 17), and (44, 9). A line falls through (0, 32) and (65, 0). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9a6d04190ab9e4af16a6c4b51f8cb80b6682aebe)

**Which point, if removed, would cause the $y$-intercept to increase the most?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The data already show a negative slope and a $y$-intercept of about $32$. There are two ways we can increase the $y$-intercept. 

-  We can make the negative slope steeper so the end near the $y$-axis pivots upward.
- We can move the entire regression line upward.

We can remove a point that is:

- rotating one end of the regression line toward itself (left end down or right end up), or
- pulling the middle of the regression line downward.

Which of the points shown is pulling the middle or an end of the regression line the most?

Point $\goldE{D}$ is far lower than the pattern would suggest, and it's to the left of the other points. So left end of the regression line gets "pulled" lower to account for this point.

The line pivots around the approximate center of the data. Removing point $\goldE{D}$ raises the end near the it. Because the point is between most of the points and the $y$-axis, the $y$-intercept increases.

With point $\goldE{D}$:

![The same scatterplot as above.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d6a60f60265fad75337ec6d268826c80263ab53c)

Without point $\goldE{D}$:

![A scatterplot with the same points as above, except without point D. A line falls through (0, 47) and (60, 0). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7e0872e5051b26e5dbd8028ffded78dff1102b21)

[[☃ explanation 1]]
If we removed point $\goldE{D}$, the $y$-intercept would increase the most.

assessment_items.question_content:
Clare was curious which points in the scatterplot below were most influential in terms of the standard deviation of the residuals.

![A scatterplot has horizontal axis, x, which ranges from 0 to 80, in increments of 20; and vertical axis, y, which ranges from negative 75 to 100, in increments of 25. Point A is graphed at (57, 13). Point B is graphed at (53, 11). Point C is graphed at (38, negative 53). Point D is graphed at (42, negative 46). 8 individual data points are plotted as follows. (57, 13), (53, 11), (38, negative 53), (42, negative 46), (47, negative 10), (62, 45), (41, negative 37), and (44, negative 30). A line rises through (35, negative 51), (50, negative 2) and (65, 47). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/099abd5e3b9db77363f38353d699312cfb19b1cd)

**Which point, if removed, would cause the standard deviation of the residuals $s$ to increase the most?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The standard deviation of the residuals $s$ measures the size of a typical residual. In other words, it tells us the typical vertical distance between each point and the regression line.

We can remove a point that is close to the regression line to increase this typical distance.

Which of the points shown, if removed, would increase this typical distance the most?

Relative to the other points, $\maroonD{C}$ has a very small residual since it is close to the regression line. Removing it would make the typical residual larger.

A larger typical residual means that $s$ would increase.

With point $\maroonD{C}$:

![The same scatterplot as above.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/38a3c0b07fbdde7369de1b5e56177ff5f22d0f54)

Without point $\maroonD{C}$:

![A scatterplot with the same points as above, except without point C. A line rises through (35, negative 50) and (75, 75). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c088e3e356592b4e6725c91639f5284cb6538d38)

[[☃ explanation 1]]
If we removed point $\maroonD{C}$, the standard deviation of the residuals would increase the most.

assessment_items.question_content:
Flavia was trying to describe point A's influence on $r^2$ in the scatterplot below.

![A scatterplot has horizontal axis, x, which ranges from negative 5 to 80, in increments of 10; and vertical axis, y, which ranges from 0 to 100, in increments of 20. Point A is plotted at (54, 36). 15 individual data points are plotted as follows. (55, 58), (52, 77), (40, 48), (43, 38), (48, 74), (60, 87), (42, 62), (45, 55), (46, 77), (43, 53), (67, 81), (31, 25), (51, 85), (51, 65), and (62, 90). A line rises through (20, 19) and (60, 79). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bfc7b957c7549b62037650fda93a8f5c8bf0dbd0)

Flavia said, "Point A has a very large negative residual compared to the rest of the points, so it's both an outlier and a high-leverage point. Removing it would strengthen the overall pattern, so $r^2$ would increase."

**What is the first mistake in Flavia's response?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Outlier

An outlier on a scatterplot has a relatively larger residual than the other points have. Point $A$ is farther below the regression line than the other points.

So Flavia is correct that point $A$ is an outlier.

###High-leverage point

A high-leverage point on a scatterplot has a relatively larger or smaller $x$-value than the other points have. Point $A$ has an $x$-value that is close to the mean $x$-value.

So Flavia is *incorrect* that point $A$ is a high-leverage point.
Flavia's first mistake is that point A is not a high-leverage point.

choices.content: Point A is not an outlier.
Point A is not a high-leverage point.
Removing this point would weaken the overall pattern, not strengthen it.
Removing this point would decrease $r^2$, not increase it.

assessment_items.question_content:
Johann was trying to describe point $A$'s influence on the $y$-intercept in the scatterplot below.

![A scatterplot has horizontal axis, x, which ranges from negative 90 to 5, in increments of 10; and vertical axis, y, which ranges from negative 20 to 80, in increments of 20. 1 large point is plotted at (negative 82, 41). 11 individual data points are plotted as follows. (negative 43, 31), (negative 47, 46), (negative 62, 56), (negative 58, 45), (negative 53, 54), (negative 38, 37), (negative 59, 59), (negative 56, 50), (negative 55, 59), (negative 59, 53), and (negative 29, 19). A line falls through (negative 80, 60) and (0, 18). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cca05f1302d93be329b83bea694395d56a539b4d)

Johann said, "Point $A$ has a very large negative residual and a very low $x$-value compared to the rest of the points, so it's both an outlier and a high-leverage point. Removing it would raise the left end of the regression line, so the $y$-intercept would increase."

**What is the first mistake in Johann's response?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Outlier

An outlier on a scatterplot has a relatively larger residual than the other points have. Point $A$ is farther below the regression line than the other points.

So Johann is correct that point $A$ is an outlier.

###High-leverage point

A high-leverage point on a scatterplot has a relatively larger or smaller $x$-value than the other points have. Point $A$ has an $x$-value that is far less than the mean $x$-value.

So Johann is correct that point $A$ is a high-leverage point.

###Regression line

Point $A$ is far lower than the pattern would suggest, so the left end of the line gets "pulled" lower to account for this point.

The line pivots around the approximate center of the data. Removing the low point raises the end near the low point.

So Johann is correct that the removing point $A$ would raise the left end of the regression line.

###$y$-intercept

Because most of the other points are between point $A$ and the $y$-axis, removing the low point raises the end near the low point and lowers the end near the $y$-axis. The $y$-intercept decreases.

So Johann is *incorrect* that the $y$-intercept would increase.
Johann's first mistake is that removing point $A$ would decrease the $y$-intercept, not increase it.

choices.content: Point $A$ is not a high-leverage point.
Removing this point would lower the left end of the regression line, not raise it.
Removing this point would decrease the $y$-intercept, not increase it.

assessment_items.question_content:
Damien was trying to describe point $A$'s influence on the slope in the scatterplot below.

![A scatterplot has horizontal axis, x, which ranges from negative 30 to 30, in increments of 10; and vertical axis, y, which ranges from negative 30 to 30, in increments of 10. 1 large point is plotted at (negative 22, 8). 11 individual data points are plotted as follows. (7, negative 1), (3, 7), (negative 12, negative 13), (negative 8, negative 16), (negative 3, 3), (12, 15), (negative 9, negative 5), (negative 6, negative 7), (negative 5, 4), (negative 9, negative 9), and (21, 16). A line rises through (negative 20, negative 9) and (25, 15). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9e836d93aaccb836cb43e3c8f44b346631d53e79)

Damien said, "Point $A$ has a very large positive residual and a very low $x$-value compared to the rest of the points, so it's both an outlier and a high-leverage point. Removing it would raise the left end of the regression line, so the slope would become less steep."

**What is the first mistake in Damien's response?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Outlier

An outlier on a scatterplot has a relatively larger residual than the other points have. Point $A$ is farther above the regression line than the other points.

So Damien is correct that point $A$ is an outlier.

###High-leverage point

A high-leverage point on a scatterplot has a relatively larger or smaller $x$-value than the other points have. Point $A$ has an $x$-value that is far less than the mean $x$-value.

So Damien is correct that point $A$ is a high-leverage point.

###Regression line

Point $A$ is far higher than the pattern would suggest, so the left end of the line gets "pulled" higher to account for this point.

The line pivots around the approximate center of the data. Removing the high point lowers the end near the high point.

So Damien is *incorrect* that the removing point $A$ would raise the left end of the regression line.
Damien's first mistake is that removing point $A$ would lower the left end of the regression line, not raise it.

choices.content: Point $A$ is not a high-leverage point.
Removing this point would lower the left end of the regression line, not raise it.
Removing this point would make the slope steeper, not less steep.

assessment_items.question_content:
Allie was trying to describe point A's influence on $r$ in the scatterplot below.

![A scatterplot has horizontal axis, x, which ranges from 0 to 80, in increments of 15; and vertical axis, y, which ranges from negative 70 to 0, in increments of 20. 1 large point is plotted at (75, negative 61). 11 individual data points are plotted as follows. (47, negative 47), (43, negative 32), (28, negative 26), (32, negative 36), (37, negative 25), (52, negative 39), (31, negative 23), (34, negative 31), (35, negative 21), (31, negative 28), and (61, negative 54). A line falls through (0, negative 1) and (60, negative 50). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ecb8007c99220d216890ee0097d4db2f1aa1c6ce)

Allie said, "Point A is far to the right compared to the rest of the points, so it's an outlier. Removing it would weaken the overall pattern, so $r$ would get closer to $0$."

**What is the first mistake in Allie's response?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Outlier

An outlier on a scatterplot has a relatively larger residual than the other points have. Point $A$ is relatively closer to the regression line than the other points.

So Allie is *incorrect* that point $A$ is an outlier.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
Allie's first mistake is that point A is not an outlier.

choices.content: Point A is not an outlier.
Removing this point would strengthen the overall pattern, not weaken it.
Removing this point would get $r$ closer to $-1$, not to $0$.
Removing this point would get $r$ closer to $1$, not to $0$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Santino was trying to describe point A's influence on the standard deviation of the residuals $s$ in the scatterplot below.

![A scatterplot has horizontal axis, x, which ranges from negative 80 to 10, in increments of 10; and vertical axis, y, which ranges from 50 to 120, in increments of 20. 1 large point is plotted at (negative 55, 115). 11 individual data points are plotted as follows. (negative 43, 85), (negative 47, 96), (negative 62, 89), (negative 58, 83), (negative 53, 98), (negative 38, 97), (negative 59, 95), (negative 56, 90), (negative 59, 90), (negative 29, 89), and (negative 72, 83). A line rises through (negative 80, 90) and (10, 99). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9637d9d955614f3ab31f863c259c3f49288087bd)

Santino said, "Point A has a very large positive residual compared to the rest of the points, so it's a high-leverage point. Removing it would strengthen the overall pattern, so the standard deviation of the residuals would decrease."

**What is the first mistake in Santino's response?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Residual

A residual is the difference $(y - \hat y)$ between the observed value and the value predicted by the regression line. Point $A$ is above the regression line, so it was higher than the predicted value.

Santino is correct that point $A$ has a positive residual.

###High-leverage point

A high-leverage point on a scatterplot has a relatively larger or smaller $x$-value than the other points have. Point $A$ has an $x$-value that is close to the mean $x$-value.

So Santino is *incorrect* that point $A$ is a high-leverage point.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
Santino's first mistake is that point A is not a high-leverage point.

choices.content: Point A is not a high-leverage point.
Removing this point would weaken the overall pattern, not strengthen it.
Removing this point would increase the standard deviation of the residuals, not decrease it.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lois was trying to describe point $A$'s influence on the $y$-intercept in the scatterplot below.

![A scatterplot has horizontal axis, x, which ranges from negative 2 to 60, in increments of 10; and vertical axis, y, which ranges from 78 to 115, in increments of 10. 1 large point is plotted at (27, 85). 11 individual data points are plotted as follows. (37, 98), (33, 106), (18, 99), (22, 96), (42, 107), (21, 105), (24, 100), (25, 102), (21, 100), (51, 99), and (8, 93). A line rises through (0, 95) and (60, 104). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a9a4b850173c8d53ead644616cbad9fa98b89f4a)

Lois said, "Point $A$ has a very large negative residual and an $x$-value near the mean of the rest of the points, so it's an outlier, but not a high-leverage point. Removing it would lower the entire regression line, so the $y$-intercept would decrease."

**What is the first mistake in Lois's response?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Outlier

An outlier on a scatterplot has a relatively larger residual than the other points have. Point $A$ is farther below the regression line than the other points.

So Lois is correct that point $A$ is an outlier.

###High-leverage point

A high-leverage point on a scatterplot has a relatively larger or smaller $x$-value than the other points have. Point $A$ has an $x$-value that is near the mean $x$-value.

So Lois is correct that point $A$ is not a high-leverage point.

###Regression line

This point is far lower than the pattern would suggest, and it's close to the mean $x$-value. So the entire line (not just one end of the line) gets "pulled" lower to account for this point.

Removing this point would let the line raise higher to fit the overall pattern of the remaining points.

So Lois is *incorrect* that the removing point $A$ would lower the regression line.
Lois's first mistake is that removing point $A$ would raise the regression line, not lower it.

choices.content: Removing this point would raise the regression line, not lower it.
Removing this point would increase the $y$-intercept, not decrease it.
